text: The surgeon begins the operation by mobilizing the liver, carefully taking down adhesions to free the organ for further surgical access.
The surgeon uses a robotic hook and bipolar craspers to take down the left coronary and triangular ligaments, facilitating further mobilization of the liver.
The surgeon uses robotic instruments to take down the falciform ligament, extending the dissection up to the hepatocarpal confluence.
The surgeon carefully performs dissection near the left hepatic vein, ensuring no injury occurs to this vital structure.
The surgeon elevates the left lateral section of the liver superiorly, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon sharply dissects and removes adhesions from the inferior surface of the liver, continuing the mobilization process.
The robotic arm four, equipped with a biograsper, assists in the precise dissection of the liver's surrounding structures.
The surgeon opens the orenseous ligament along its trajectory, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon opens the gastrohepatic ligament, carefully inspecting for any accessory left hepatic artery that may supply the left hemiliver.
The liver is visibly elevated in a cephalad direction, revealing its anatomical positioning and surrounding structures.
The surgeon uses a hook instrument to sharply dissect and remove adhesions from the falciform ligament.
The surgeon uses energized scissors to biopsy a small subcapsular lesion on the liver, with the specimen sent for frozen section analysis.
An intraoperative liver ultrasound is performed, revealing the presence of a lesion in segment three of the liver.
The surgeon examines the liver, marking the transection line for liver extension to ensure the removal of the liver lesion with negative margins.
The surgeon exposes the base of the umbilical vein by applying lateral traction to the future remnant and medial traction to the left lateral section of the liver.
The surgeon enters the base of the umbilical vein to achieve inflow vascular control, aiming to minimize bleeding during the operation.
The surgeon uses a hook cautery to dissect the left portal vein branches supplying segments two and three of the liver.

The surgeon uses straight vascular bulldog clamps to achieve inflow vascular control, placing one clamp on the left portal vein and another on the left hepatic artery branches supplying segments two and three of the liver.
The surgeon begins superficial transection of the liver parenchyma using monopolar scissors cautery after achieving complete inflow vascular control.
The assistant holds the laparoscopic suction device to evacuate smoke and blood from the liver parenchymal surface during the procedure.
The surgeon uses an extended vessel sealer to divide the deeper liver parenchyma, carefully navigating around the crossing vascular branches to prevent bleeding.
The surgeon carefully inserts the vessel sealer into the liver tissue, examining the area closely to avoid damaging vascular structures.
The surgeon carefully observes the movement of the liver parenchyma to ensure there is no resistance during the placement or insertion of the vessel sealer.
The surgeon removes the bulldogs and uses a laparoscopic linear vascular stapler to transect the portal pedicle leading to liver segments two and three, including the baldach, in an N-block fashion.
The surgeon uses a 60mm laparoscopic linear stapler to transect the portal pedicle, ensuring precise division of the liver parenchyma while avoiding injury to crossing vascular branches.
The surgeon staples along the orensis ligament, progressing toward the stump of the left hepatic vein.
The left hepatic vein is clearly visualized during the liver transection, with minimal bleeding observed in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to transect the superficial liver parenchyma, revealing the stump of the left hepatic vein.
The surgeon fires a second linear vascular stapler to transect the left hepatic vein, positioned flush to the inferior vena cava along the rancid ligament.
The surgical team completes the transection of the left hepatic vein using a laparoscopic linear stapler, with no significant bleeding observed during the procedure.
The surgeon fires a third stapler to complete the liver resection, targeting the remaining part of the left hepatic vein, with minimal bleeding observed.
The surgeon achieves hemostasis of the superficial liver parenchyma using a bipolar robotic instrument, ensuring minimal bleeding in the surgical field.
A large endoscopic retrieval bag is inserted into the abdomen to remove the liver specimen. The resection surface of the liver appears dry, with no visible bleeding or bile leak.
The assistant uses suction to actively evacuate smoke from the surgical field, ensuring clear visibility for the ongoing procedure.
The surgeon administers a mark and injection to the abdomen, likely for post-operative pain management.
The surgeon closes the port sites under direct laparoscopic visualization, following a standard closure technique.
